items.html:
Why inclusion matters

LGBTQ+ stands for lesbian, gay, bisexual, trans and queer or questioning, and the plus sign includes everyone whose sexual orientation or gender identity sits outside what has often been treated as the norm. The people you support are as varied as anyone else, and some of them are lesbian, gay, bisexual, trans or queer. Inclusive care simply means every person receives care that is dignified, respectful and shaped around who they truly are.

  • Dignity for all: everyone has the right to be treated with respect and to have their identity valued.
  • Care shaped around the person: good care fits the individual rather than an assumption about them.
  • A basic right, not a favour: inclusion is part of the care you owe every person, not an optional extra.

What this course is — and is not

This course raises your awareness of LGBTQ+ people’s experiences and helps you offer inclusive, person-centred care. It explains the law, respectful language and everyday good practice, so you can support people with warmth and confidence and challenge discrimination when you see it.

It is knowledge-level learning only. It does not replace your organisation’s equality, diversity and inclusion policies, its safeguarding procedures, or the supervised, hands-on training your role requires. Where this course and your employer’s policies differ, always follow your organisation’s policies and ask your manager if you are unsure.

The Equality Act 2010

The Equality Act 2010 is the law that protects people from discrimination. It sets out nine protected characteristics, and it is against the law to discriminate against someone because of any of them. Two of these characteristics are directly relevant here: sexual orientation and gender reassignment. As a care worker delivering a service, you and your organisation must not treat anyone unfairly because of who they are.

  • Sexual orientation: protects people who are lesbian, gay, bisexual or heterosexual.
  • Gender reassignment: protects people who are proposing to undergo, are undergoing or have undergone a process to reassign their sex.
  • A legal duty: the protection applies to the care and services you provide, not just to employment.

What the protection means in practice

A trans person is protected under gender reassignment from the point they decide to live in their affirmed gender. They do not need to have had any medical treatment or surgery, and they do not need a Gender Recognition Certificate, to be protected. The law also protects people from being treated unfairly because they are thought to be LGBTQ+, or because someone they are connected to is.

The Care Quality Commission (CQC) expects services to meet these duties as part of treating people with dignity and respect. Inclusive, equal care is therefore both a legal requirement and a mark of good, well-led care.

items.question: Which two protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010 most directly protect LGBTQ+ people from discrimination in care?

answers.text: Sexual orientation and gender reassignment.
answers.feedback: Correct. Sexual orientation and gender reassignment are the two protected characteristics most directly relevant to LGBTQ+ people.

answers.text: Age and disability.
answers.feedback: Age and disability are protected characteristics, but they are not the ones that most directly protect LGBTQ+ people.

answers.text: Marriage and religion only.
answers.feedback: Marriage or civil partnership and religion or belief are protected too, but the key characteristics here are sexual orientation and gender reassignment.

answers.text: None — the Act does not cover LGBTQ+ people.
answers.feedback: The Act clearly protects LGBTQ+ people through sexual orientation and gender reassignment.

items.html:
Why history shapes trust

Many older LGBTQ+ people grew up at a time when their identity was criminalised, treated as an illness, or something they could lose their home, job or family over. Sex between men was only partly decriminalised in England and Wales in 1967, and for years afterwards prejudice remained widespread and openly expressed. People learned to hide who they were simply to stay safe.

  • A lifetime of caution: some people spent decades concealing their identity to avoid harm.
  • Hard-won trust: past experiences can make it difficult to trust services and carers.
  • Real fear of going back: some worry that needing care means hiding who they are once again.

The worker’s role

Understanding this history helps you see why a person might be guarded, or might fear that being open will lead to poorer treatment. Some older people who were once open about their identity feel they have to go “back into the closet” when they start receiving care, because they are unsure how they will be received.

Your role is to be someone they can trust. By showing through your words and manner that you accept people exactly as they are, you help undo years of caution. Small signals of acceptance — a warm response, no awkwardness, no judgement — tell a person that they are safe with you.

items.question: Because of the discrimination many older LGBTQ+ people experienced earlier in life, some fear having to hide their identity again when they start receiving care.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Past experiences of criminalisation and prejudice can make people fear having to conceal who they are, so building trust matters.
items.wrongMsg: Actually this is true. Many older LGBTQ+ people lived through criminalisation and prejudice, and some fear hiding their identity again when they need care.

items.html:
Two different things

Sexual orientation and gender identity are often confused, but they are separate parts of who a person is. Keeping them clear in your mind helps you understand and support people accurately, and use the right language.

  • Sexual orientation: who a person is emotionally or romantically attracted to — for example lesbian, gay, bisexual or heterosexual.
  • Gender identity: a person’s own inner sense of their gender — whether they are a man, a woman, both, neither or something else.
  • Independent of each other: knowing one tells you nothing about the other.

Understanding trans identities

Someone is transgender, or trans, when their gender identity is different from the sex they were recorded as at birth. A trans woman was recorded male at birth and lives as a woman; a trans man was recorded female at birth and lives as a man. Some people are non-binary, meaning they do not identify simply as a man or a woman. A trans person may be of any sexual orientation, just as anyone else may be.

You do not need to fully understand every identity to give good care. What matters is that you respect how each person describes themselves and take your lead from them.

items.question: What is the difference between sexual orientation and gender identity?

answers.text: Sexual orientation is who a person is attracted to; gender identity is a person’s own sense of their gender.
answers.feedback: Correct. Orientation is about attraction, and gender identity is about a person’s inner sense of who they are. They are separate.

answers.text: They mean the same thing and can be used interchangeably.
answers.feedback: They are two different things. Orientation is about attraction, while gender identity is about a person’s sense of their own gender.

answers.text: Sexual orientation applies only to trans people.
answers.feedback: Everyone has a sexual orientation, and everyone has a gender identity. Neither applies only to trans people.

answers.text: Gender identity tells you who a person is attracted to.
answers.feedback: It does not. A trans person, like anyone, may be of any sexual orientation. The two are independent.

items.html:
Respectful, current language

Language matters because it shows respect and helps people feel safe. Terms change over time, and the best guide is always the words a person uses about themselves. Use those same words back to them.

  • Follow their lead: mirror the language a person uses to describe their own identity and relationships.
  • Keep it current: avoid old or clinical terms that many people find offensive or outdated.
  • Ask, don’t guess: if you are unsure what someone prefers, ask politely and privately.

Names and pronouns

Using a person’s correct name and pronouns is one of the simplest and most powerful ways to show respect. Pronouns are the words we use in place of someone’s name, such as “he”, “she” or “they”. Always use the name and pronouns a person tells you are theirs, even if they differ from the records or from the name they were given at birth.

If you slip up, simply correct yourself, apologise briefly and move on — there is no need to make a fuss. Repeatedly using the wrong name or pronoun on purpose is disrespectful and can be a form of harassment.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: You should always use a person’s correct *name* and *pronouns*, taking your lead from the words they use about themselves. You should never *assume* someone’s identity, and if you are unsure it is respectful to *ask* politely and privately.

items.html:
Never assume

It is easy to make assumptions without realising it — to assume a woman has a husband, or that an older person could not possibly be gay, bisexual or trans. Assumptions like these can make a person feel invisible and unable to be open. Inclusive care starts with keeping an open mind and letting people tell you who they are.

  • No default: do not assume anyone is heterosexual or that their gender matches their records.
  • Use open questions: ask about “partners” rather than assuming a husband or wife.
  • Let people share at their pace: give people the space to be open, without pressure.

Confidentiality about identity

A person’s sexual orientation and gender identity are private and sensitive information. If someone shares this with you, it is not yours to pass on. You should never disclose that a person is LGBTQ+ to their family, other people they know, or colleagues who have no need to know, without the person’s clear agreement.

Revealing someone’s identity without their consent is called “outing” them, and it can cause real harm — damaging relationships, or exposing someone to prejudice or danger. Only share such information with colleagues where it is genuinely necessary for the person’s care and where the person is content for you to do so.

items.question: A person you support tells you, in confidence, that she is a lesbian. Her daughter later asks you directly whether her mother has ever had a girlfriend. What should you do?

answers.text: Keep the information confidential and do not disclose her identity without her clear consent.
answers.feedback: Correct. A person’s identity is private. Disclosing it without consent would be outing her, which can cause real harm.

answers.text: Tell the daughter the truth, as family have a right to know.
answers.feedback: They do not. This is private information, and sharing it without consent could cause serious harm to the person’s relationships and safety.

answers.text: Hint at it so the daughter can work it out for herself.
answers.feedback: Hinting still discloses private information the person shared in confidence. Keep it confidential unless she agrees you may share.

answers.text: Note it in the daily log for all staff to read.
answers.feedback: Only share such information where it is genuinely necessary for care and the person agrees. Broadcasting it to all staff is not appropriate.

items.html:
Making no assumptions

Person-centred care means seeing each person as an individual and building support around their wishes, relationships and history — not around what you expect them to be. When you make no assumptions, people feel able to be themselves, which is the foundation of good care.

  • Start with the person: ask what matters to them and how they wish to be supported.
  • Record what they tell you: capture their preferences so care stays consistent across the team.
  • Keep an open, curious mind: be ready to learn about the person rather than filling in the gaps yourself.

A welcoming environment

People quickly sense whether a place is welcoming. Small, visible signals show LGBTQ+ people that they will be accepted. Inclusive-care guidance from organisations such as Stonewall, the NHS and Skills for Care encourages services to make inclusion visible and to use language that does not assume everyone is heterosexual.

  • Inclusive language: use words like “partner” on forms and in conversation.
  • Visible signals: welcoming imagery or symbols can quietly reassure people they are safe here.
  • A consistent culture: every member of the team helps set a respectful, accepting tone.

items.question: Which of these best helps create a welcoming, inclusive environment for LGBTQ+ people?

answers.text: Using inclusive language such as “partner” and making no assumptions about anyone’s identity.
answers.feedback: Correct. Inclusive language and an open, assumption-free approach help people feel safe to be themselves.

answers.text: Assuming everyone is heterosexual unless they say otherwise.
answers.feedback: This assumption can make LGBTQ+ people feel invisible and unwelcome. Keep an open mind instead.

answers.text: Waiting for people to prove their identity before treating them respectfully.
answers.feedback: Respect is never conditional. Everyone deserves inclusive, dignified care from the outset.

answers.text: Avoiding the subject entirely so no one is offended.
answers.feedback: Silence can feel unwelcoming. Using inclusive language and being openly accepting is far more reassuring.

items.html:
Relationships and chosen family

Family and close relationships matter deeply to wellbeing. For some LGBTQ+ people, the people closest to them are not blood relatives but a “chosen family” of trusted friends and partners, sometimes because they became estranged from their birth family. These bonds deserve the same respect and welcome as any other family.

  • Respect who matters to them: welcome partners, friends and chosen family as the person wishes.
  • Do not judge relationships: treat same-sex partners exactly as you would any other couple.
  • Support connection: help people stay in touch with the people who matter most to them.

Faith and culture

People hold many different beliefs, and a person may be both LGBTQ+ and a person of faith. Their identity and their culture or religion are both important parts of who they are, and both deserve respect. Your own personal or religious views must never affect the care you give or lead you to treat someone as less deserving of dignity.

If you ever feel a tension between your beliefs and your duty to care inclusively, remember that your role is to support every person equally. Talk to your manager or supervisor for guidance rather than letting it affect the person’s care.

Personal care and dignity

Personal care is intimate, and for trans people it can feel especially exposing if it is not handled with sensitivity. Every person has the right to privacy, dignity and control over their own body and care. The way to get this right is the same as for anyone: ask the person about their preferences and follow their lead.

  • Ask, do not assume: check how the person wishes to be supported and by whom.
  • Respect their body and choices: follow their wishes about washing, dressing and personal items.
  • Protect privacy: maintain dignity at every step and never expose or examine out of curiosity.

Getting it right for trans people

A trans person may use aids such as a binder, padding or a prosthesis as part of who they are, and these should be treated with the same respect as any other personal item. Continue to use the person’s correct name and pronouns throughout personal care. Never ask intrusive questions about someone’s body or medical history out of curiosity, as this is undignified and can be deeply distressing.

If you are genuinely unsure how best to support someone, ask them respectfully and privately what they would prefer. Being led by the person is always the safest and most dignified approach.

items.question: When providing personal care for a trans person, it is good practice to ask them privately about their preferences and follow their lead, rather than making assumptions.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Asking respectfully and being led by the person protects their dignity, privacy and control over their own care.
items.wrongMsg: Actually this is true. Asking privately about preferences and following the person’s lead is the most dignified, person-centred approach.

items.html:
Accurate, respectful records

Good records help the whole team give consistent, respectful care. Recording how a person wishes to be addressed means they should not have to explain themselves over and over to each new worker. Record what the person tells you factually and respectfully, and keep sensitive information secure.

  • Record their name and pronouns: note the name and pronouns the person uses, so every worker gets it right.
  • Capture their wishes: record preferences about care, relationships and who may be involved.
  • Keep it secure and shared appropriately: only those involved in the person’s care should see sensitive details.

The Accessible Information Standard

The Accessible Information Standard is an NHS standard that requires health and care services to identify, record and meet people’s communication needs, and to share those needs with others involved in their care where appropriate. The same spirit applies to recording how a person wishes to be addressed and supported.

Getting records right also means being careful with historical documents. A trans person’s previous name should not be shared or referred to unnecessarily, as this can cause distress. Always check that the records you use reflect who the person is now, and correct them respectfully if they do not.

Forms of discrimination

Discrimination is not always obvious. The Equality Act describes several forms, and recognising them helps you spot when something is wrong and needs challenging or reporting.

  • Direct discrimination: treating someone worse because they are, or are thought to be, LGBTQ+.
  • Indirect discrimination: a rule or practice that applies to everyone but unfairly disadvantages LGBTQ+ people.
  • Harassment: unwanted behaviour that violates a person’s dignity or creates a hostile or degrading environment.

Microaggressions and everyday slights

Not all harm comes from obvious hostility. Microaggressions are the small, often unintended comments and behaviours that chip away at a person’s dignity — a disbelieving reaction, a joke at their expense, or repeatedly “forgetting” someone’s pronouns. On their own each may seem minor, but over time they leave people feeling unwelcome and unseen.

Being aware of microaggressions helps you avoid them in your own practice and notice them in others. Treating every person consistently, with the same warmth and respect, is the best safeguard against these everyday slights.

items.question: A colleague keeps making “jokes” about a resident being gay and repeatedly uses the wrong pronouns for a trans resident. How is this best described?

answers.text: Harassment and microaggressions that undermine the residents’ dignity and must be addressed.
answers.feedback: Correct. Unwanted jokes and deliberately using the wrong pronouns are harassment and microaggressions, and they need to be challenged and reported.

answers.text: Harmless banter that no one should worry about.
answers.feedback: It is not harmless. This behaviour undermines dignity and can amount to harassment under the Equality Act.

answers.text: Acceptable, as long as the residents do not complain.
answers.feedback: Discrimination is wrong whether or not someone complains. Many people stay silent out of fear, so you should still act.

answers.text: A private matter between the colleague and the residents.
answers.feedback: This affects people’s dignity and safety in your service. It is everyone’s responsibility to challenge and report it.

items.html:
Responding to discriminatory comments

When you hear a discriminatory or offensive remark, staying silent can feel easier, but silence can seem like agreement. You can challenge comments calmly and professionally without causing a scene. Standing up for inclusive care protects the people you support and sets the tone for the whole team.

  • Challenge the comment, not the person: make clear the remark is not acceptable, calmly and respectfully.
  • Support anyone affected: check on the person targeted and reassure them.
  • Report it: record what happened and tell your manager, following your organisation’s policy.

Supporting someone who is coming out

“Coming out” is when a person chooses to share their sexual orientation or gender identity. It can take courage, especially for someone who has hidden it for years. If a person comes out to you, respond warmly and calmly, thank them for trusting you, and let them know it makes no difference to the respect and care they receive.

Follow the person’s lead about who else, if anyone, they wish to tell. It is their information to share, in their own time. Never take it upon yourself to tell others, and never pressure someone to come out before they are ready.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: If you hear a discriminatory remark, you should *challenge* it calmly and professionally, and *report* it through your organisation’s policy. If a person comes out to you, respond with *warmth* and follow their *lead* about who else they wish to tell.

items.html:
Never out anyone

The single most important rule when supporting LGBTQ+ people is to respect their privacy. Whether someone has just come out to you or has been open for years, it is always their choice who knows about their identity. Passing that information on without their consent — outing them — can damage relationships and put people at risk.

  • Consent first: only share someone’s identity with their clear agreement.
  • Need-to-know only: where sharing is genuinely necessary for care, keep it to those directly involved.
  • When in doubt, keep it private: if you are unsure, do not disclose — ask the person and your manager.

Where to get advice

You are not expected to have every answer. If you are unsure how to support someone, or you have witnessed discrimination, there is always help available. Speak to your manager or supervisor, use supervision and team meetings, and follow your organisation’s equality, inclusion and safeguarding policies. Trusted organisations such as Stonewall, Skills for Care and the NHS also provide guidance on inclusive care.

This course has raised your awareness so you can offer inclusive, person-centred and dignified care to lesbian, gay, bisexual, trans and queer or questioning people, and challenge discrimination when you see it. It is knowledge-level learning only. It does not replace your organisation’s equality and inclusion policies, its safeguarding procedures, or the supervised training your role requires. If you are ever unsure, ask — and always be led by the person about who they are and how they wish to be supported.

items.question: You are unsure how best to support a resident who has told you, in confidence, that he is gay and is worried about how staff will treat him. What is the best course of action?

answers.text: Keep his confidence, reassure him, and seek advice from your manager or your organisation’s policies without outing him.
answers.feedback: Correct. Respect his privacy, reassure him, and get guidance through the proper channels without disclosing his identity to others.

answers.text: Tell the rest of the team so they can “keep an eye out” for him.
answers.feedback: That would out him without consent. Only share where genuinely necessary for care and with his agreement.

answers.text: Do nothing, as it is not your concern.
answers.feedback: His worry is a real concern. Reassure him and seek advice, while keeping his confidence.

answers.text: Encourage him to come out to everyone so it is out in the open.
answers.feedback: Coming out is always the person’s own choice, in their own time. Never pressure anyone to do so.
